title: Phase diagram of the extended chequerboard $J-Q$ model
abstract: The chequerboard $J-Q$ model was proposed to describe the direct phase transition from the antiferromagnetic (AFM) state to the plaquette-sin glet (PS) solid state observed in SrCu$_2({\rm BO}_3)_2$. In this paper, we present a Monte Carlo study of the ground state of an extended ve rsion of this model. For all parameters investigated, we find only a direct first-order phase transitions from the AFM to the PS phase, with no intermediate phase between them. On the transition line, the system exhibits an emergent $O(4)$ symmetry. Furthermore, we find that the Bi nder ratio of the columnar valence-bond solid state can be used to locate the phase transition. It exhibits a monotonic finite-size scaling b ehavior, allowing for a precise determination of the transition point.
summary: This work uses Quantum Monte Carlo simulations on the extended chequerboard J-Q model to map its phase diagram. The authors demonstrate that the transition from the Antiferromagnetic (AFM) state to the Plaquette-Singlet (PS) solid is universally first-order for $\lambda < 1$. Crucially, they establish that this transition is governed by an emergent O(4) symmetry.
main_problem: The study investigates the ground state properties and phase diagram of an extended chequerboard J-Q model to understand the nature of the direct transition from the Antiferromagnetic (AFM) state to the Plaquette-Singlet (PS) solid state, relevant to materials like SrCu2(BO3)2.
main_result: The research concludes that for parameters $\lambda < 1$, the system undergoes a direct first-order phase transition between AFM and PS phases, exhibiting an emergent O(4) symmetry. No intermediate phase was found across the investigated parameter space.
method: Large-scale Stochastic Series Expansion (SSE) Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations were employed to map the ground state phase diagram.
model_or_system: The model is an extended chequerboard J-Q Hamiltonian, incorporating nearest-neighbor Heisenberg interactions ($J$) and four-spin couplings ($Q, \lambda Q$) on different plaquettes. It interpolates between known limits like the original CBJQ model ($\lambda=0$) and the $J-Q2$ model ($\lambda=1$).
key_observables: Binder ratios ($U_s, U_p, U_{VBS}$), order parameters ($	ext{m}_p, 	ext{m}_s$), and probability distributions $P(D_x, D_y)$ are used to diagnose phase transitions and symmetries.
important_parameters: The coupling constants $Q$ and $\lambda$, which define the parameter space $(Q, \lambda)$, are crucial. Specific limits include $\lambda=0$ (O(4) symmetry) and $\lambda=1$ (O(5) symmetry).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that Binder ratio crossing points provide a reliable method for locating phase transitions via finite-size scaling, although computational limitations sometimes restrict the focus to certain ratios.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of binder ratios ($U_{VBS}$) on $\lambda$ across various values. Other figures show order parameter distributions $P(D_x, D_y)$ confirming O(4) symmetry for $\lambda < 1$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the model by first establishing the Hamiltonian and relevant physical context (AFM to PS transition). It then applies QMC simulations across varying coupling parameters ($\lambda$) using diagnostic tools like Binder ratios. The core of the work is mapping the phase diagram, confirming the nature (first-order) and symmetry (O(4)) of the primary transition.
why_interesting: The detailed study of emergent symmetries (O(4) vs O(5)) in a lattice model provides deep insight into how underlying microscopic interactions dictate macroscopic quantum behavior, which is fundamental to understanding strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The extended CBJQ model. Black lines denote the Heisenberg interactions of strength J. The red Q-plaquettes, denoted as FP, represent the four-spin interactions of strength Q, and the blue λQ-plaquettes, denoted as EP, represent the four-spin interactions of strength λQ, with λ ≤1.
FIG. 3. Binder ratios Us and Up of the extended CBJQ model versus Q for different system sizes at (a) λ = 0.1 and (b) λ = 0.9.

FIG. 2. Phase diagram of the extended model in the (Q, λ)- plane. Transitions in this phase diagram are all first-order. The black and blue points are selected estimates from previous studies. The solid line is guide for eye.

FIG. 4. Critical points extracted from the crossing points of Us(L) and Us(2L). The dashed lines for λ ≤0.8 are obtained from individual fits, while the dashed line at λ = 0.9 is ob- tained from a joint fit.

FIG. 5. Squared order parameters m2 s and m2 p plotted versus 1/L at the transition point Qc for (a) λ = 0.1 and (b) λ = 0.9. The inset in (b) shows a zoom of the largesystemsize region. Solid lines represent the fit results.
